coredata.dc:title: Study of parameters affecting the degradation of rhodamine-B and methyl orange dyes by Annona muricata leaf extract synthesized nanoparticles as well as their recyclability
coredata.dc:creator: Velidandi, Aditya
Pabbathi, Ninian Prem Prashanth
Baadhe, Rama Raju
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a facile and green approach has been employed for the degradation of toxic dyes like rhodamine-B (rh-B) and methyl orange (MO) individually as well as in combination (rh-B+MO) in presence of sodium borohydride (NaBH4). The silver-silver chloride nanoparticles (Ag-AgCl NPs) fabricated using aqueous Annona muricata leaf extract were used as nano-catalysts. Effect of reaction parameters such as nano-catalyst dosage, NaBH4 dosage, temperature and pH were investigated to achieve maximum catalytic efficiency. The maximum degradation was obtained at 100 μg of Ag-AgCl NPs (mass of nano-catalyst), 25 mg of NaBH4 (mass of reducing agent) and 60 °C (temperature). pH 8, 6 and 6 showed maximum degradation for rh-B, MO and rh-B+MO dyes respectively. The experimental data obtained was following the pseudo-first order reaction kinetics. About 96.79 ± 2.61% (0.7485 min−1), 97.51 ± 2.14% (1.0542 min−1) and 97.08 ± 2.63% (0.3474 min−1) degradation was observed for rh-B (under 5 min), MO (under 4 min) and rh-B+MO (under 10 min) dyes respectively. Recyclability studies showed that Ag-AgCl NPs can be recycled and reused up to Cycle 5 (51.11 ± 3.34%) for rh-B, Cycle 6 (47.35 ± 3.19%) for MO and Cycle 3 (45.53 ± 2.32%) for rh-B+MO combination.

head.ce:title: Study of parameters affecting the degradation of rhodamine-B and methyl orange dyes by Annona muricata leaf extract synthesized nanoparticles as well as their recyclability

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, National Institute of Technology, Warangal, Telangana, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biotechnology, National Institute of Technology, Warangal, Telangana, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a facile and green approach has been employed for the degradation of toxic dyes like rhodamine-B (rh-B) and methyl orange (MO) individually as well as in combination (rh-B+MO) in presence of sodium borohydride (NaBH4). The silver-silver chloride nanoparticles (Ag-AgCl NPs) fabricated using aqueous Annona muricata leaf extract were used as nano-catalysts. Effect of reaction parameters such as nano-catalyst dosage, NaBH4 dosage, temperature and pH were investigated to achieve maximum catalytic efficiency. The maximum degradation was obtained at 100 μg of Ag-AgCl NPs (mass of nano-catalyst), 25 mg of NaBH4 (mass of reducing agent) and 60 °C (temperature). pH 8, 6 and 6 showed maximum degradation for rh-B, MO and rh-B+MO dyes respectively. The experimental data obtained was following the pseudo-first order reaction kinetics. About 96.79 ± 2.61% (0.7485 min−1), 97.51 ± 2.14% (1.0542 min−1) and 97.08 ± 2.63% (0.3474 min−1) degradation was observed for rh-B (under 5 min), MO (under 4 min) and rh-B+MO (under 10 min) dyes respectively. Recyclability studies showed that Ag-AgCl NPs can be recycled and reused up to Cycle 5 (51.11 ± 3.34%) for rh-B, Cycle 6 (47.35 ± 3.19%) for MO and Cycle 3 (45.53 ± 2.32%) for rh-B+MO combination.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past few decades, rapid and uncontrollable increase of the global population in combination with technological advancements in the sectors of industry and agriculture has greatly affected the environment and human health [1–3]. Increase in the number of industries is also a major contributor affecting the overall worldwide environment [1,4–12]. Among all the sources for the environmental pollution, textile industries are by far the major contributors due to the application of several toxic dyes [13]. Effluents released from the textile industries consists a vast number of intermediate compounds due to the involvement of several unit operations [14]. Occurrence of several impurities (such as surfactants, biocides, pesticides and polyvinyl alcohol) also increases the salinity of the water [15]. Additionally, commercial dyes consisting of azo functional groups can generate by-products upon oxidation and hydrolysis which can be mutagenic and toxic leading to the increase in color intensity of the textile wastewater [1,16]. The application of various synthetic dyes in several industries such as pharmaceutical, pulp printing, tannery, textile, paper and temporary tattoos produces adversely toxic chemical compounds as well as colored industrial effluents resulting in the contamination of both ground and surface water [1,17]. Adversely, biodegradation of these dyes is highly difficult which is mainly because of their properties like complicated aromatic structures, synthetic origin, highly photolytic and chemically stable [1,18–20].

In literature, several physical (like membrane filtration, ion exchange and adsorption), chemical (like photocatalytic oxidation, Ozonation, Fenton oxidation and coagulation) and biological (like anaerobic and aerobic) approaches have been reported for the removal of toxic textile dyes and their resulting by-products from industrial effluents as well as from wastewater [1,21–23]. However, the major drawbacks of these approaches are (i) not economical, (ii) not efficient and lastly (iii) difficulty in the transformation of products from one phase to another without any destruction [1]. Several research groups have also reported the application of chemically synthesized metal nano-composites as efficient heterogeneous catalysts such as MnO2−MWCNT, nano ZnO, CuS quantum dots, sulfonated-phenylacetic acid coated Fe3O4, CuO@mTiO2@CF, SiO2 CuCl2, ZnS (as pure, doped, and decorated) and graphene quantum dots [24–33]. However, fabrication of these metal nano-composites is very difficult, low reusability and recyclability, adverse reaction conditions, costlier, inadequate yield, high stacking as well as very low product conversion rate [1]. In order to overcome these drawbacks, metal or metal-oxide NPs (via physicochemical approaches) are employed as nano-catalysts in the degradation of textile dyes because of their properties such as (i) cost effective, (ii) superior catalytic ability and (iii) high recyclability, rigidity as well as stability [1]. Application of metal or metal-oxide NPs is a viable choice for the degradation of textile dyes in wastewater [34–36]. Additionally, these are also employed in other applications such as drug delivery, antimicrobial coating, bio-imaging, hydrogen storage and generation, catalysis, optoelectronics, fuel as well as solar cells [1,22]. Exploring the NPs properties is a new and emerging field of science due to their applicability in diverse fields.

Degradation of textile dyes by the application of NPs offers a major advantage over conventional approaches due to its fast and efficient degradation without the formation of toxic polycyclic compounds as by-products [22]. As per existing literature, various metal or metal-oxide NPs such as TiO2, ZnO, CuO, Au, Ag, Cu and Fe have gained importance by demonstrating their catalytic potential in the degradation of several textile dyes such as alizarin red, methyl orange, gentian violet dye, methylene blue (MB), congo red (CR), rhodamine-B, etc. [37–41]. Nonetheless, based on the available literature it has been observed that the dye degradation process is slow in comparison with the wastewater generated from various dye industries [22]. Most of the metal or metal-oxide NPs reported as nano-catalysts have a degradation rate limiting parameter such as low dye concentration, increased temperature, H2O2 concentration, radiation, etc. [22]. Additionally, NPs synthesis using various physicochemical approaches has major drawbacks such as (i) particles symmetry, (ii) involvement of toxic chemicals and solvents, (iii) adverse synthesis conditions and ultimately (iv) compatibility with the environment [17]. Hence, fabrication of NPs as nano-catalyst with superior particles symmetry and eco-friendly approach is much needed.

Among the various green synthesis approaches, application of plant extracts in the fabrication of metal NPs such as Ag, CdTe, ZnO, ZnTe, etc. has gained significant momentum [42–51]. Biogenic synthesis is by far the most accessible, easiest and safest approach developed for the synthesis of NPs [17,51]. Phyto-constituents present in the plant extracts act as reducing, capping and stabilizing agents [17]. Various research reports and reviews are available emphasizing the application of plant extracts synthesized NPs as nano-catalyst for the degradation of several textile dyes [46–51]. It is observed that in most of the studies reported, either the dye degradation rate was very slow or the dye concentration used was very less (Table 1 ). However, we believe that superior degradation rate can be achieved even at high dye concentrations.

Based on the above information, in this work we have focused on (i) application of aqueous A. muricata leaf extract synthesized Ag-AgCl NPs (Figure S1) as nano-catalyst for the degradation of textile dyes such as rh-B and MO (Table 2 ) individually as well as in combination in the presence of sodium borohydride (NaBH4) as reducing agent, (ii) studying the effect of various parameters on dye degradation process such as Ag-AgCl NPs (nano-catalyst) concentration, NaBH4 (reducing agent) concentration, temperature and pH of the system, and finally (iii) recyclability of Ag-AgCl NPs was also investigated.

Rhodamine-B, methyl orange, sodium borohydride, hydrochloric acid (HCl) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were procured from Hi-media Laboratories. Preparation of stock solutions, buffers and necessary dilutions were made using sterile double-distilled water (dd. H2O). pH of the solutions were adjusted accordingly using 1 N HCl and 1 M NaOH stock solutions. All dye degradation experiments were performed in stirring water bath. Dye solutions were centrifuged (15,000 rpm and 5 min) and diluted (40x for rh-B and 20x for MO) accordingly before measuring the absorbance (554 nm for rh-B and 464 nm for MO) using Ultraviolet-Visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-1800).

Catalytic activity of the bio-fabricated Ag-AgCl NPs for the degradation of textile dyes such as rh-B and MO in the presence of NaBH4 was done based on the method already published [51]. Briefly, 5 mL of dye solution (50 mg/100 mL stock solution) was mixed with 5 mg of NaBH4 followed by 10 μL of Ag-AgCl NPs solution (2 mg/mL stock solution) and incubated at 37 °C. pH of the reaction was maintained at 7. At regular intervals dye solutions were centrifuged, diluted and absorbance was recorded at 554 and 464 for rh-B and MO dyes respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of various parameters on dye degradation process
ce:section.ce:para:
To study the effect of various parameters in the degradation of rh-B and MO dyes, four influencing factors were considered such as Ag-AgCl NPs (as nano-catalyst) concentration, NaBH4 (as reducing agent) concentration, temperature and pH of the reaction system.
i
The effect of Ag-AgCl NPs concentration on the dye degradation was studied by varying the concentrations from 20, 40, 60, 80 and 100 μg. NaBH4 concentration was fixed at 5 mg, whereas temperature and pH of the system was maintained at 37 °C and 7 respectively.
ii
To determine the effect of NaBH4 concentration on the dye degradation, varying concentrations such as 5, 15 and 25 mg were used. Temperature and pH of the reaction system was maintained at 37 °C and 7 respectively. Whereas, Ag-AgCl NPs concentration was used as per previous experiment.
iii
Effect of the temperature on the reduction of rh-B and MO dyes were studied by varying the temperature of the reaction system from 40, 50 and 60 °C. pH was maintained at 7. Whereas, Ag-AgCl NPs and NaBH4 concentrations were used as per previous experiments.
iv
To study the influence of pH in the degradation of dyes, pH of the system was adjusted to 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 and 12. Temperature, Ag-AgCl NPs and NaBH4 concentrations were used as per previous experiments.

Recyclability of the Ag-AgCl NPs as nano-catalyst was determined by employing the optimized conditions in the degradation of rh-B and MO dyes individually and in combination (rh-B+MO). Recyclability experiments were performed with 100 mL dye solution (50 mg/100 mL stock solution). At the end of each degradation cycle, dye solution was centrifuged to remove the Ag-AgCl NPs, then obtained pellet was re-suspended in 20 mL dd. H2O followed by sonication for 15 min (in case the adsorption of dye molecules onto the surface of Ag-AgCl NPs). This step was repeated until the colorless solution was obtained. At the end, pellet was dried (70 °C) overnight and reused for the dye degradation. At regular intervals absorbance of the dye solution was recorded [22].

The dye degradation percentage (%) was calculated using the Eq. (1) [51].

The dye degradation kinetics were evaluated under the assumption that the NaBH4 concentration remains uniform throughout the dye reduction reaction as the concentration of reducing agent (NaBH4) used was much higher than that of the dye employed and thus they obey pseudo-first order reaction kinetics according to the Langmuire-Hinshelwood model [21,61]. Pseudo-first order reaction was represented by the Eq. (2) [21,61].

where k is the pseudo-first order rate constant, A0 is the absorbance of dye solution at ‘0' min and At is the absorbance at ‘t’ min
By plotting the graph with ln[A0 ] vs time ‘t’ min gives a straight line going downwards with slope ‘k’, which is pseudo-first order rate constant [21,61].

Experimental results obtained by performing the experiments in triplicates were presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD). SD was represented as error bars in the graphs. All the statistical analyses as well as graphical representations of the data were done using MS-Excel 2010. Band gap energy was plotted using OriginPro 2021.

The bang gap energy of the Ag-AgCl NPs was determined from the UV–Vis absorbance spectra by employing Tauc's equation (Eq. (3)).

where α is absorption coefficient, h is Planck's constant, ν is speed of light, B is constant and Ebg is band gap energy

From Eq. (3), by plotting the graph between “(αhν)2″ on X-axis and “hν” on Y-axis followed by extrapolating a straight line from the linear part of the graph onto the energy axis gives the band gap energy of the Ag-AgCl NPs (Fig. 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of various parameters on dye degradation process
ce:section.ce:para: Studying the effect of various parameters on the dye degradation is a much needed process which can increase the dye degradation rate and reducing the time taken for the complete degradation of dye. This helps in achieving a cost effective, rapid, efficient and superior dye degradation process. Details about the effect of studied parameters (such as Ag-AgCl NPs concentration, NaBH4 concentration, temperature and pH) in the degradation of rh-B and MO dyes individually and in combination (rh-B+MO) were presented as Tables 3 , 4 and 5 respectively.

Determining the adequate nano-catalyst concentration is a necessary parameter which can affect the entire dye degradation process. NaBH4 is a well-known strong reducing agent. However, it alone cannot degrade the rh-B and MO textile dyes [46,61]. Hence, it requires an intermediate (catalyst) for the transfer of electrons from borohydride ions (BH4 −) to the textile dyes. Upon transfer of electrons, the colored textile dyes will be reduced to colorless products [62]. Fig. 2 a-b, 2f-g and 2k-l represents the degradation percentages of rh-B (under 25 min), MO (under 20 min) individually and in combination (rh-B+MO dyes under 40 min) at various Ag-AgCl NPs concentrations (20, 40, 60, 80 and 100 μg) respectively.

At the maximum nano-catalyst concentration (100 μg), rh-B and MO dyes were reduced by 97.57 ± 1.74% (25 min) and 97.92 ± 1.48% (20 min) with the rate constant of 0.1495 min−1 and 0.1964 min−1 respectively. Whereas in combination (rh-B+MO), 96.91 ± 1.28% (40 min) degradation was observed at degradation rate 0.0800 min−1. Fig. 2c, h and m represents the ln(At ) vs time ‘t’ graph which provides the dye degradation rate ‘k’ for individual rh-B, MO dyes and in combination (rh-B+MO) at various nano-catalyst concentrations respectively. From Fig. 2e, j and o it was evident that as the concentration of the nano-catalyst increases the amount of time taken for the dye degradation was reduced greatly. For rh-B dye, the time was reduced from 60 min at 20 μg of Ag-AgCl NPs to 25 min at 100 μg of Ag-AgCl NPs (Fig. 2d). For MO dye, the time was reduced from 45 min at 20 μg of Ag-AgCl NPs to 20 min at 100 μg of Ag-AgCl NPs (Fig. 2i). Whereas in combination (rh-B+MO), the time was reduced from 75 min at 20 μg of Ag-AgCl NPs to 40 min at 100 μg of Ag-AgCl NPs (Fig. 2n).

As reducing agent, NaBH4 dissociates into Na+and BH4 −ions by dissolving in water. These BH4 −ions then supply the electrons which are utilized in the dye degradation [62]. Hence, determining the adequate amount of reducing agent to be used is necessary. Fig. 3 a-b, f-g and k-l represents the degradation percentages of rh-B (under 15 min), MO (under 15 min) individually and in combination (rh-B+MO dyes under 25 min) at various NaBH4 concentrations (5, 15 and 25 mg) respectively.

At the highest reducing agent concentration (25 mg), rh-B and MO dyes were reduced by 96.35 ± 1.18% (15 min) and 97.83 ± 1.64% (15 min) with the rate constant of 0.2254 min−1 and 0.2704 min−1 respectively. Whereas in combination (rh-B+MO), 98.29 ± 1.44% (25 min) degradation was observed at degradation rate 0.1416 min−1. Fig. 3c, h and m represents the ln(At ) vs time ‘t’ graph which provides the dye degradation rate ‘k’ for individual rh-B, MO dyes and in combination (rh-B+MO) at various reducing agent concentrations respectively. From Fig. 3e, j and o it was observed that as the amount of the NaBH4 increases the total time taken for the degradation of the dyes both individually and in combination was decreased significantly. For rh-B dye, the time taken was reduced from 25 min at 5 mg of NaBH4 to 15 min at 25 mg of NaBH4 (Fig. 3d). For MO, the time taken was reduced from 20 min at 5 mg of NaBH4 to 15 min at 25 mg of NaBH4 (Fig. 3i). Whereas, in combination (rh-B+MO), the time was reduced from 40 min at 5 mg of NaBH4 to 25 min at 25 mg of NaBH4 (Fig. 3n).

Temperature is another key parameter which affects the dye degradation process [1]. Therefore, studying the effect of temperature and also to determine the suitable temperature for the dye degradation is essential. Fig. 4 a-b, f-g and k-l represents the degradation percentages of rh-B (under 8 min), MO (under 6 min) individually and in combination (rh-B+MO dyes under 14 min) at various temperatures (40, 50 and 60 °C) respectively.

At the maximum temperature (60 °C), rh-B and MO dyes individually were degraded by 97.09 ± 2.06% (8 min) and 98.08 ± 1.73% (6 min) with the rate constant of 0.4540 min−1 and 0.6259 min−1 respectively. Whereas in combination (rh-B+MO), 96.85 ± 2.06% (14 min) degradation was observed at the degradation rate 0.2291 min−1. Fig. 4c, h and m represents the ln(At ) vs time ‘t’ graph which provides the dye degradation rate ‘k’ for individual rh-B, MO dyes and in combination (rh-B+MO) at various process temperatures respectively. From Fig. 4e, j and o it was observed that as the reaction temperature increases the time taken for the degradation of dyes both individually and in combination were reduced significantly. For rh-B dye, the time taken was reduced from 14 min at 40 °C to 8 min at 60 °C (Fig. 4d). For MO, the time taken was reduced from 14 min at 40 °C to 6 min at 60 °C (Fig. 4i). Whereas, in combination (rh-B+MO), the time was reduced from 22 min at 40 °C to 14 min at 60 °C (Fig. 4n).

pH is an essential factor which plays a significant part in the dye degradation process by affecting the molecular structure of the dyes through degree of ionization. It also greatly influences the nature of the nano-catalyst by affecting its surface charge, Ag-AgCl NPs aggregation and hydroxyl radicals concentration [1,21,23]. Hence, determining the suitable pH for the degradation of rh-B and MO dyes individually as well as in combination (rh-B+MO) is necessary. Fig. 5 a-b, 5f-g and 5k-l represents the degradation percentages of rh-B (under 5 min), MO (under 4 min) individually and in combination (rh-B+MO dyes under 10 min) at various pH (2, 4, 6, 8, 10 and 12) respectively.

At pH 8, rh-B dye was degraded by 96.79 ± 2.61% (5 min) with the rate constant of 0.7485 min−1 and MO dye was degraded by 97.51 ± 2.14% (4 min) with the rate constant of 1.0542 min−1 at pH 6. Whereas, pH 6 showed degradation by 97.08 ± 2.63% (10 min) with the rate constant of 0.3474 min−1 for the combination of rh-B and MO dyes (Fig. 5n). Fig. 5c, h and m represents the ln(At ) vs time ‘t’ graph which provides the dye degradation rate ‘k’ for individual rh-B, MO dyes and in combination (rh-B+MO) at various pH respectively. From the Fig. 5e it was evident that among the various pH tested only pH 6 (97.22 ± 1.83%), 8 (96.79 ± 2.61%) and 10 (97.11 ± 2.02%) showed total degradation whereas, pH 12 (72.58 ± 3.82%) showed partial degradation for rh-B dye. From the Fig. 5j it was observed that only pH 6 (97.51 ± 2.14%), 8 (96.79 ± 2.61%) and 10 (97.33 ± 1.52%) showed complete degradation for MO dye. In combination (rh-B+MO), only pH 6 (97.08 ± 2.63%) showed complete degradation which can be observed from Fig. 5o. The time taken for the total degradation of rh-B dye at pH 6, 8, 10 and 12 were 9, 5, 11 and 20 min respectively (Fig. 5d). The time taken for the total degradation of MO dye at pH 6, 8 and 10 were 4, 7 and 15 min respectively (Fig. 5i).

For practical applications in the textile dyes degradation process, the most significant attributes are recyclability, reusability and efficiency of the nano-catalyst [21]. Therefore, to determine the recyclability of the Ag-AgCl NPs as nano-catalysts and its efficiency in between the cycles, recycling experiments were performed at the above observed conditions. For rh-B dye, Cycle 1 showed similar degradation as Cycle 0. From Cycle 2 to Cycle 4 the degradation percentage has been decreased from 90.82 ± 2.79% to 72.94 ± 3.11%, whereas Cycle 5 showed only 51.11 ± 3.34% degradation under 5 min (Fig. 6 a-c). The time taken for the total rh-B dye degradation was also increased gradually from 6 min (Cycle 2) to 10 (Cycle 4), whereas Cycle 5 took around 30 min (Fig. 6e). For MO dye, Cycles 0, 1 and 2 showed similar degradation pattern. From Cycle 3 to Cycle 5 the degradation percentage has been reduced from 93.22 ± 2.37% to 69.77 ± 2.18%, whereas Cycle 6 showed only 47.35 ± 3.19% degradation under 4 min (Fig. 6f-h). The amount of time taken for the complete MO dye degradation was also increased slowly from 5 min (Cycle 3) to 9 min (Cycle 5), whereas Cycle 6 has taken 25 min to complete (Fig. 6j).

In the dye combination (rh-B+MO), only Cycle 1 has shown good degradation activity with 86.40 ± 2.08%, whereas degradation percentage was steeply reduced from Cycle 2 to Cycle 3 from 72.11 ± 3.23% to 45.53 ± 2.32% under 10 min (Fig. 6k-m). The time taken for the complete dye degradation for Cycle 0 was 10 min, whereas Cycle 1 has taken 15 min. However, Cycle 2 and Cycle 3 have taken 25 min and 45 min to complete respectively (Fig. 6o). Fig. 6d, i and n represents the ln(At ) vs time ‘t’ graph which provides the dye degradation rate ‘k’ for individual rh-B, MO dyes and in combination (rh-B+MO) at various Cycles respectively. The data obtained during the recyclability studies of Ag-AgCl NPs as nano-catalyst in the degradation of rh-B and MO dyes individually as well as in combination was presented in Table 6 . Comparison of the Ag-AgCl NPs recyclability as nano-catalysts in the degradation of textile dyes with those reported in literature was presented in Table 7 .

Presence of the NPs in the reaction solution can make the degradation process kinetically viable by reducing the activation energy required for dye reduction [17,62,66]. The redox potential of the Ag-AgCl NPs should be present in between the redox potential of NaBH4 (reducing agent, donor of electrons) and the organic textile dyes (rh-B and MO, acceptors of electrons) [17,62]. The dye degradation process in presence of NaBH4 by using NPs as nano-catalysts consists of two stages [67–69]: (i) dissociation of NaBH4 into Na+ and BH4 −, absorption of electrons donated by BH4 − ions on to the NPs surface, charge formation and surface trapping as well as generation of free radicals from water (H2O) and oxygen (O2) molecules by redox reactions. Free radicals like hydroxyl (OH−), anionic superoxide (O2 −) and hydroperoxyl radicals (HO2 −) are generated [1,22,70]. After the addition of NaBH4 into the aqueous system, it immediately dissociates thus reducing its reductive strength. Since BH4 −is highly nucleophilic, it exhibits higher potential to offer electrons much required for dye degradation reactions [1,22]. (ii) Second stage is associated with surface reactivity and followed by the adsorption of dyes onto NPs surface and degradation of organic textile dyes by free radicals. Dye molecules are electrophilic in nature and readily react with free radicals [22]. Thus resulting in the degradation of chromophore structures of rh-B and MO dyes to form simple molecules (such as CO2 and H2O) [22,55,71,72].

Hence, in order to validate the role of Ag-AgCl NPs as nano-catalyst for the degradation of rh-B and MO in presence of NaBH4, experiments were performed with only NaBH4 (without Ag-AgCl NPs) as well as with only Ag-AgCl NPs (without NaBH4). However, no degradation was observed in both the cases. This states that neither Ag-AgCl NPs nor the NaBH4 alone has the potential to degrade the rh-B/MO dyes. Thus Ag-AgCl NPs acted as efficient nano-catalysts by reducing the rh-B and MO dyes by efficiently relaying the electrons from NaBH4 (donors) to rh-B/MO dyes (acceptors) [73,74]. Fig. 7 presents the possible mechanism involved in the Ag-AgCl NPs catalysed degradation of rh-B and MO dyes in presence of NaBH4 (as reducing agent).

The occurrence of toxic textile dyes in the aquatic environment has attracted significant research interest because of its adverse health effects on the humans and other living organisms as well as the total environmental impact. The present work was focused on determining the effect of various parameters in the degradation of two such textile dyes (rh-B and MO). It demonstrated that the catalytic activity of Ag-AgCl NPs was dependent on various parameters such as temperature, pH, nano-catalyst and reducing agent concentration. After the optimization process, the time taken for the complete degradation of the rh-B (60 min to 5 min) and MO (45 min to 4 min) dyes individually as well as in combination (75 min to 10 min) was significantly reduced. In addition, Ag-AgCl NPs as nano-catalyst has shown good recyclability and reusability. These Ag-AgCl NPs can be good alternatives for the degradation of toxic textile dyes as nano-catalyst.
